MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice And Motion Of Defendants Dharmesh Patel And Sixteenth Street Hotel Llc For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Jan-22-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Kimberly Levy, Esq. 415-421-2800 of Dolan Law Firm for Plaintiff.
Mark Wilson, Esq. 415-217-6990 of Manning Kass Ellrod Ramirez Trester LLP for Defendant DHARMESH PATEL, 16TH STREET HOTEL LLC, SIXTEENTH STREET HOTEL, LLC.

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant argues negligence. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT DHARMESH PATEL, 16TH STREET HOTEL LLC, SIXTEENTH STREET HOTEL, LLC'S (ERRONEOUSLY SUED) MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendants Dharmesh Patel, 16th Street Hotel LLC, amd Sixteenth Street Hotel, LLC's motion for summary judgment is denied. Plaintiff has identified facts-the absence of a second handrail and defendants' failure to provide adequate illumination of the stairway-from which a reasonable jury could infer that a dangerous condition existed and was a substantial factor in bringing about Mr. O'Connell's injuries. In Buehler v. Alpha Beta Co. (1990) 224 Cal.App.3d 729, in contrast, defendant met its burden of proof on summary judgment to show the absence of a dangerous condition. Harpke v. Lankershim Estates (1951) 103 Cal.App.2d 143, 145, which held that no inference of negligence arises from the mere proof of a fall upon a stairway, is distinguishable on that ground and because it did not involve summary judgment. Triable issues of material fact also exist as as to whether defendants' alleged violations of San Francisco Building Code 504 (g) and 802(c) were the proximate cause of plaintiff's injuries under a negligence per se theory. (O'Connell Depo. pp. 90, 92, 94; O'Connell verified responses to interrogatories, Jessup Decl. Exh. 4, Set One (1), Nos. 15, 21). Causation in fact ordinarily presents a question of fact which cannot be resolved on summary judgment. Vasquez v. Residential Investments, Inc. (2004) 118 Cal.App.4th 269, 288-289.(as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)
